summary: DeVault documents the bloated state of modern web development by dissecting a New York Times article page that required 2.8 MB and 748 HTTP requests to deliver 9 KB of actual content. He advocates for conservative web development principles: minimal JavaScript, optimized images, CSS-driven interactivity, and server-side rendering. He compares sr.ht's lean pages against GitHub's considerably heavier equivalents to demonstrate these principles in practice. The post concludes with practical recommendations for reducing web page bloat and a pointed jab at Google's AMP as a self-serving solution to a problem the industry should solve through better engineering.
key_points: A NYT article page delivered 2.8 MB across 748 requests for 9 KB of actual content — only 8% of the download served the reader
sr.ht's pages are an order of magnitude lighter than GitHub's equivalent pages, demonstrating that feature-rich web apps don't require massive payloads
Single-page apps offload rendering costs onto visitors' devices and batteries rather than genuinely saving resources
Progressive enhancement — designing without JavaScript first, then adding it sparingly — produces faster, more accessible sites
Google's AMP is a self-serving faux standard when the real solution is better web development practices
money_quotes: The text of the article is a total of 9037 bytes, including the title, author, and date.
8% of the total download was dedicated to the content.
5 discrete external companies were informed of my visit to the page and given the opportunity to run arbitrary JavaScript on it.

Pushing the complexity of rendering your single-page app into the frontend might save you miniscule amounts of server-side performance, but you'd just be offloading the costs onto your visitor's phone and sucking their battery dry.
You can build beautiful, interactive websites with HTML and CSS alone, supported by a simple backend.
Design your website to be usable without JavaScript, and use small amounts of it to enhance the experience - rather than to be the experience.
And if anyone at Google is reading, you should try recommending these strategies for speeding up pages instead of pushing self-serving faux standards like AMP.
key_insight: Modern web pages are grotesquely bloated because developers offload complexity to the client; disciplined server-side rendering and minimal JavaScript can deliver the same features at a fraction of the cost.
